label: National Palace (Palacio Nacional)
sub: Presidential residence and seat of executive power; site of Sheinbaum's daily press conferences (mañaneras). Location of constitutional oath-taking ceremony on October 1, 2024.

label: Chamber of Deputies (San Lázaro)
sub: Where Morena's supermajority coalition passed the judicial reform, autonomous agency dissolution, social program constitutionalization, and energy nationalization amendments throughout late 2024.

label: Senate of the Republic
sub: Site of the November 28, 2024 vote dissolving INAI, COFECE, and five other autonomous constitutional agencies. Senate protesters broke into the building during judicial reform debates in September 2024.

label: Supreme Court (SCJN)
sub: Reconfigured September 1, 2025 with 9 justices (reduced from 11), all elected in the June 2025 judicial elections and perceived as Morena-aligned. Hugo Aguilar Ortiz serves as new Chief Justice.

label: National Electoral Institute (INE)
sub: Oversaw the historic June 2025 judicial elections for 2,681 judicial posts. Absorbed some transparency oversight functions after INAI's dissolution in late 2024.

label: Secretariat of Security (SSPC)
sub: Federal security and intelligence coordination headquarters under Secretary Omar García Harfuch. December 2024 reform made SSPC coordinating body of the National Intelligence System with expanded criminal investigation powers.

label: U.S. Embassy, Mexico City
sub: Located on Paseo de la Reforma. Normal diplomatic protocols with Ambassador Ken Salazar resumed on October 2, 2024 after AMLO-era suspension. Site of major bilateral coordination meetings on tariffs, deportations, and security.

label: San Ysidro – Tijuana POE
sub: World's busiest land port of entry. Primary processing point for Mexico's mass deportation reception program (México te Abraza). 10,000 National Guard troops deployed nearby in Feb 2025 to pause tariff threats.

label: Nogales Border Crossing
sub: Key US-Mexico port of entry in Sonora/Arizona corridor. Active National Guard deployment zone as part of Sheinbaum's February 2025 border security surge. On March 27, 2026, a small group of U.S. military personnel briefly crossed into Mexican territory here; Sheinbaum called it an unintentional incident reversed immediately, ruling out a sovereignty violation.

label: Ciudad Juárez – El Paso POE
sub: Major crossing point for both migration and commercial trade. One of nine border reception centers established by Mexico under the México te Abraza deportee repatriation program.

label: Nuevo Laredo – Laredo POE
sub: Single largest commercial cargo crossing in the entire US-Mexico border, processing over $300 billion in annual trade. Key pressure point during 25% tariff threat in early 2025; USMCA-compliant goods remained exempt.

label: Matamoros – Brownsville (Rio Grande Water Dispute Zone)
sub: Located in the Rio Grande Valley agricultural zone at the center of the 1944 water treaty dispute. Texas cited $994M in crop losses from Mexico's shortfall. A 5% tariff was imposed December 9, 2025 and lifted after December 12 agreement.

label: Monterrey — Premier Nearshoring Hub
sub: Mexico's industrial capital and leading nearshoring destination. Home to the Hofusan Industrial Park (40+ Chinese companies) and clusters in automotive, logistics, and ICT. Anchor of the Nuevo León industrial corridor targeted by Plan México's 15 special economic zones.

label: Querétaro — Aerospace and Advanced Manufacturing Hub
sub: Major aerospace and advanced manufacturing cluster in the Bajío region. Significant nearshoring expansion during 2024–2025; part of the industrial corridor targeted by Plan México to reduce Chinese import dependence.

label: Guadalajara — 'Silicon Valley of Mexico'
sub: Mexico's ICT and tech services hub. Also the base of operations for CJNG, which is headquartered in Jalisco state. The CJNG Teuchitlán training camp was discovered 50km west of the city in March 2025.

label: Teuchitlán, Jalisco — CJNG Rancho Izaguirre Camp
sub: Discovered March 5, 2025 by missing persons volunteers: ~200 pairs of shoes, 3 makeshift crematoriums, charred remains, and military training infrastructure. Mayor José Murguía Santiago arrested May 3, 2025 on organized crime and forced disappearance charges.

label: Culiacán, Sinaloa — Cartel Civil War Ground Zero
sub: Capital of Sinaloa state and ground zero of the Chapitos vs. Mayiza faction civil war that erupted after El Mayo Zambada's July 2024 U.S. arrest. Over 2,197 homicides since the conflict began; state homicide rate up approximately 400%.

label: Chilpancingo, Guerrero — High Violence Capital
sub: State capital of Guerrero, one of Mexico's most violent states. Security analysts describe parts of the region as 'near-ungovernable' due to cartel territorial control. Key data point for debates about the 'hugs not bullets' security policy.

label: Saltillo, Coahuila — Automotive Production Hub
sub: Home to major GM and Chrysler/Stellantis production facilities. Key beneficiary of USMCA auto content rules and nearshoring investment surge during 2024–2025. Part of the northern industrial corridor targeted by Plan México.

label: Tijuana — Electronics & Medical Devices Manufacturing
sub: Major hub for electronics, medical devices, and aerospace manufacturing due to US proximity. Dual function as a commerce zone and deportee reception center under México te Abraza. Part of Plan México's special economic zone framework.

label: Aguascalientes — Nissan & Auto Parts Hub
sub: Home to Nissan's major Mexican production facility and a dense network of auto parts suppliers. Benefiting from USMCA rules of origin requirements driving nearshoring investment. Included in Plan México's industrial zone initiative.

label: Guanajuato — Industrial Hub Under Security Pressure
sub: Mexico's most homicide-affected state in absolute numbers (2,539 in 2025). Home to major automotive and agricultural industries targeted by Plan México nearshoring policy. The CJNG-CSRL conflict has persisted despite federal security operations.

label: Chiapas — Southern Border Migration Zone
sub: Key entry point for Central American migration into Mexico, with proximity to Guatemala. National Guard deployments to Chiapas were part of Mexico's February 2025 border surge to prevent migrants from reaching the US-Mexico border. Major processing zone under México te Abraza.

label: Dos Bocas Refinery (Tabasco)
sub: Pemex's flagship new refinery — the crown jewel of AMLO's energy sovereignty agenda. On March 24, 2026, an explosion triggered an oil spill, prompting a federal emergency response and FGR investigation. Environmental groups raised concerns about marine ecosystem damage. The incident reignited debate over the $17-billion public investment and Pemex's operational safety record.

label: Estadio Banorte (formerly Azteca) — World Cup 2026 Venue
sub: Reopened March 28, 2026 after ~2 years of World Cup renovation works. Mexico drew Portugal 0-0 in the inaugural friendly match. The renamed stadium is one of three Mexican FIFA 2026 venues alongside Guadalajara's Estadio Akron and Monterrey's Estadio BBVA. Protesters gathered outside over Mexico's 130,000+ missing persons and stadium renovation costs.

label: Morelia, Michoacán — CJNG Expansion Zone / Plan Michoacán
sub: State capital of Michoacán, a key theater of CJNG territorial expansion. On March 31, 2026, Sheinbaum announced 'Plan Michoacán for Peace and Justice' — a 57-billion-peso, 12-point strategy — after street protests in Morelia and Uruapan demanding security improvements. Michoacán remains one of six states with rising homicide counts.

label: Uruapan, Michoacán — Security Protests
sub: Michoacán's second-largest city and a major avocado-producing hub with persistent CJNG presence. Street protests here and in Morelia demanding security improvements in March 2026 directly triggered Sheinbaum's announcement of Plan Michoacán on March 31, 2026. Also the city where Morena senators chanted support for Raúl Morón during Mayor Grecia Quiroz's Senate visit — an incident Sheinbaum was pressed to condemn as political gender violence.

label: AIFA Airport (Santa Lucía) — Train Link Delayed
sub: Felipe Ángeles International Airport — built at the military airbase of Santa Lucía as AMLO's flagship infrastructure project. The Buenavista–AIFA suburban train link (43-minute ride) was repeatedly delayed, most recently past Holy Week March 29, 2026 due to missing safety certification. No new opening date confirmed as of April 1; inauguration must occur before FIFA World Cup 2026.
